PageResearchNote.description:
LLM output for per-page comprehension during agentic search.

Each page read during the search loop is summarized into a research note
capturing key findings, perspectives covered, and knowledge contribution.

summary.description: 150-200 word research summary focused on findings relevant to query
summary.title: Summary

perspectives.description: STORM perspectives covered by this page
perspectives.title: Perspectives

key_facts.description: 3-5 specific facts/data points with numbers, dates, or named entities
key_facts.title: Key Facts

knowledge_contribution.description: What new understanding this page adds to the research
knowledge_contribution.title: Knowledge Contribution

PageResearchNote.title: PageResearchNote

schema_json.description: Batch wrapper for page research notes with invalid-note filtering.

notes.description: Research notes for each page read
notes.title: Notes

schema_json.title: PageSummaryBatch

prompt:
You are a research analyst reading web pages for a deep research project.

RESEARCH QUESTION: What are the current best-available estimates of permafrost carbon release under RCP 4.5 and RCP 8.5 scenarios by 2100?

Read the following 5 pages and create a research note for each.

--- PAGE: [DOC] attachment_1.docx - Regulations.gov ---
URL: https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx
CONTENT:
Title: CEQ-2022-0005-0277_attachment_1.docx URL Source: https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx Published Time: Fri, 11 Apr 2025 22:33:54 GMT Number of Pages: 5 Markdown Content: ![Image 1](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/580eee11-c1f3-4559-ba93-9d6802fd7852_html_e070e311b0776305.png) **Woodwell Climate Research Center****•****Arctic Initiative at Harvard Kennedy School****•****Alaska Institute for Justice** _Comment submitted by Permafrost Pathways at Woodwell Climate Research Center in response to the_ _National Environmental Policy Act Guidance on Consideration of Greenhouse Gas Emissions and Climate Change_ _88 Fr 1196_ This comment on the “National Environmental Policy Act Guidance on Consideration of Greenhouse Gas Emissions and Climate Change” (hereinafter “Guidance”) is submitted on behalf of the Permafrost Pathways project at Woodwell Climate Research Center. Woodwell Climate Research Center (“Woodwell Climate”) is a non-profit organization based in Falmouth, Massachusetts that comprises research scientists and policy experts who work with partners worldwide to understand the impacts of and to address climate change on a global scale. Woodwell Climate scientists helped to launch the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change in 1992 and shared the Nobel Prize awarded to the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) in 2007. The organization continues to have global impact, bringing together cutting-edge science and translating scientific advances into climate policy solutions. In April 2022, the Arctic program at Woodwell Climate launched [Permafrost Pathways](https://permafrost.woodwellclimate.org/), a new initiative funded through the Audacious Project, that is dedicated to amplifying efforts to collect the best data on Arctic carbon emissions, contextualize this information within global emissions budgets, and transform this science into actionable policy recommendations. Permafrost Pathways represents a collaboration between Woodwell Climate and the Arctic Initiative at Harvard Kennedy School, the Alaska Institute for Justice, the Alaska Native Science Commission, and ten Alaska Native tribes that are experiencing immediate impacts of permafrost thaw. Permafrost is ground that has been continually frozen for at least two consecutive years and often for thousands of years. Permafrost extends across the boreal and tundra biomes and in mountain regions across the globe, underlying roughly 38% of Alaska’s land area.[1](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote1sym) As global temperatures rise, this once-frozen ground is beginning to thaw, creating an increasingly unstable and dangerous environment that presents an immediate and localized risk to Alaska and has the potential to exacerbate climate change on a global scale. While several US federal agencies are actively involved in safeguarding the Arctic from environmental degradation and mitigating adverse effects of climate change,[2](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote2sym) most regulatory frameworks do not account for permafrost thaw. Permafrost Pathways consequently recognizes the importance of accounting for permafrost thaw as part of the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA) review process. The NEPA environmental review process is a critical tool for promoting sound decisions that are equitable, cohesive, coordinated, and informed by science. To that end, Permafrost Pathways commends the Council on Environmental Quality (CEQ) for its continued commitment to revitalizing and strengthening NEPA through regulatory revisions and publication of the Guidance. Accounting for permafrost thaw as part of this Guidance, however, is necessary to ensure that federal agencies are implementing NEPA in a manner that effectively safeguards the natural environment within the context of a changing climate. **NEPA reviews of major federal actions in Alaska have not adequately considered immediate and long-term impacts of permafrost thaw.** Among the most observable impacts of permafrost thaw is thaw-induced ground collapse, which can contribute to the destruction of infrastructure, homes, schools, medical facilities, roads, and public utilities that ensure access to electricity, clean water, and other necessities. When compounded with other environmental hazards, such as flooding and erosion, permafrost thaw can cause unparalleled damage to the built environment and cause community-wide displacement.[3](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote3sym) Despite these risks, permafrost thaw has not yet been consistently or effectively integrated into the NEPA review process for projects relating to natural ecosystems or the built environment. The implications of such exclusion on existing projects are readily apparent: for example, permafrost thaw is impacting critical military infrastructure, including at the Northern Warfare Training Center at Fort Wainwright, Alaska;[4](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote4sym) it is also well established that thawing permafrost threatens to destabilize certain sections of the Trans-Alaska Pipeline (TAP), thereby compromising its structural integrity and increasing the risk of a devastating oil spill.[5](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote5sym) Even if the implications of permafrost thaw on these projects were not readily recognized at the time of the NEPA review, NEPA provides that, for significant issues that emerge after an EIS is approved, a Supplemental EIS (SEIS) should be conducted. The best science that is available today includes new information on permafrost thaw, as well as other climate change-induced changes, such as extreme precipitation, fishery collapse, erosion, and warming water temperatures. Recently published SEIS’ for projects previously approved in Alaska do seem to reflect increased recognition of permafrost thaw: For example, the Department of Energy’s Final SEIS for the Alaska Liquefied Natural Gas Project included assessments of permafrost impact, quantification of said impact, and mitigation of further loss.[6](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote6sym) Such inclusion was likely in response to a recommendation from the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency that DOE/FERC “[d]evelop and include estimates for each greenhouse gas associated with impacts to permafrost soils (e.g., thaw/degradation) from construction activities proposed under…the Life Cycle Analysis,”[7](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote7sym) and to several public comments calling for similar consideration.[8](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote8sym) In addition, the Final EIS prepared for the Willow Master Development Plan (DOI-BLM-AK-0000-2018-0004-EIS) included an assessment of permafrost impacts as part of the “affected environment and environmental consideration” section and its review of “cumulative effects.”[9](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote9sym) Unfortunately, these projects may have already contributed to irreversible permafrost thaw. The Guidance should help to ensure timely issuance of SEIS’ that adequately consider permafrost thaw. In particular, it should lead the Army Corps of Engineers to conduct an SEIS for the proposed Donlin Gold mine project (as has been requested by federally recognized Tribes in Alaska);[10](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote10sym) and ultimately help agencies to avoid arbitrary and capricious action under NEPA, as is alleged in pending litigation over the Final EIS and Record of Decision for Lease Sale 258 in the Cook Inlet of Alaska.[11](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote11sym) **Estimated GHG emissions from permafrost thaw are “reasonably foreseeable” and should be used to assess present and future impacts, despite current exclusion from most assessments and scenarios.** The Guidance states that agencies generally should quantify reasonably foreseeable direct and indirect gross and net GHG emissions increases or reductions, both for individual pollutants and aggregated in terms of carbon dioxide equivalence by using the best available science and data. To quantify these emissions, the CEQ recommends various resources available on its GHG Accounting Tools website. Most of these tools, however, are unlikely to account for potential GHG emissions from permafrost thaw, as this phenomenon is generally missing from national and international monitoring, modeling, and assessments. Only two of 11 Earth System Models that informed the latest IPCC report (Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6, CMIP6)—which is recognized by the international community as the “best available science and data”— included permafrost;[12](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote12sym) neither of these models represented abrupt thaw, wildfire-mediated thaw, or the release of carbon from below-ground combustion during wildfire.[13](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote13sym) Assessments from the US Global Change Research Program (USGCRP) also lack comprehensive consideration of permafrost thaw and emissions from compounding events. Nevertheless, for almost a decade, the IPCC has reported with high confidence the likelihood of a permafrost carbon feedback on global climate. Experts predict that Arctic permafrost currently stores 1.4 trillion tons of carbon—or at least twice as much carbon as is currently in the Earth’s atmosphere (not accounting for carbon in subsea permafrost).[14](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote14sym) Without aggressive, near-term climate mitigation, the resulting carbon emissions could be as high as 550 Gt CO2 by 2100, on par with or even exceeding continued emissions from the United States based on current rates.[15](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote15sym) Permafrost emissions could therefore take up more than 40% of the remaining carbon budget to limit warming to 1.5°C—the threshold beyond which we expect substantially worse outcomes for permafrost, ice sheets, and coral reefs.[16](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote16sym) The Guidance should explain that scientific uncertainty as to emission levels should not preclude consideration of such emissions as part of the NEPA review process, and should caution agencies about gaps and inconsistencies in existing accounting tools. The Guidance heavily defers to agencies on the “reasonability” of foreseeable GHG emissions, and further advises agencies to “remain aware of the evolving body of scientific information;” as noted, however, the exclusion of permafrost from existing tools and models (including EPA databases and those maintained by CEQ) may create an additional burden on agencies that lack the resources and information to adequately quantify the impact of a major federal action on permafrost thaw. When considering the effects of climate change on a Proposed Action, for instance, the Guidance recognizes as informative the assessments from the USGCRP (and others), yet recent National Climate Assessments have provided only cursory consideration of permafrost thaw as a mitigation and adaptation challenge. **Agencies should remain apprised of the latest research on the potential acceleration of permafrost thaw and its foreseeable impact on GHG emissions.** Ensuring the efficacy of NEPA reviews in accounting for foreseeable GHG emissions and ultimately considering context for such emissions warrants additional consideration from the CEQ. CEQ suggests as a best practice for agencies to “where relevant,…summarize and cite to available scientific literature to help explain the real-world effects—including those that will be experienced locally in relation to the proposed action—associated with an increase in GHG emissions that contribute to climate change, such as sea-level rise, temperature changes, ocean acidity, and more frequent and severe wildfires and drought, and human health effects (including to underserved populations).” Increasingly, scientific literature and the international scientific community is recognizing that certain actions—particularly, protecting permafrost regions and the cryosphere, generally—is a matter of urgent global concern that impacts human communities and natural ecosystems that exist beyond polar and mountain regions. Integrating this analysis as part of a NEPA review, however, is unlikely to occur without more explicit guidance from CEQ. The Guidance should offer more explicit direction and clarify the expectations for disseminating emerging research on permafrost that would inform both a quantitative and qualitative assessment (as is permitted under the Guidance). To ensure that agencies are not unreasonably burdened by the need to stay apprised of the latest assessments, quantification, and modeling of emissions from permafrost thaw, the Guidance could recognize the role of the CEQ in promoting available avenues for knowledge sharing. The Interagency Agency Research Policy Committee (IARPC), for example, remains an underutilized platform through which federal agencies, non-federal researchers, and Arctic communities may engage in knowledge and information exchange relevant to NEPA reviews. The Guidance would also benefit from stressing greater urgency and responsibility of agencies to seriously consider measurable impacts of proposed actions on cumulative GHG emissions and global climate change. **NEPA should advance coordinated adaptation and resilience responses in the Arctic and promote environmental justice for Arctic communities.** Importantly, the Guidance recognizes the disproportionate risks that climate change poses to certain groups, including Tribal Nations and Indigenous communities; it could nevertheless do more to ensure a coordinated, inclusive and equitable approach to NEPA reviews. Alaska Native communities that have contributed the least to climate change are currently pursuing a range of adaptation strategies to respond to permafrost thaw, storm impacts, coastal and riverine erosion, and flooding.[17](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote17sym) Such strategies include community-led relocation, which can best occur with active involvement and funding from the US federal government. Historically, however, NEPA has not helped to facilitate such action, but has actually hindered these efforts. 

--- PAGE: News: Expert assessment of vulnerability of permafrost carbon ---
URL: https://www.globalcarbonproject.org/news/PermafrostCarbon.html
CONTENT:
Science Highlight Expert assessment of vulnerability of permafrost carbon to climate change Schuur EAG, Abbott BW, Bowden WB, Brovkin V, Camill V, Canadell JG, Chanton JP, Chapin III FS, Christensen TR, Ciais P, Crosby BT, Czimczik CI, Grosse G, Harden J, Hayes DJ, Hugelius G, Jastrow JD, Jones JB, Kleinen T, Koven CD, Krinner G, Kuhry P, Lawrence DM, McGuire AD, Natali SM, O'Donnell JA, Ping CL, Riley WJ, Rinke A, Romanovsky VE, Sannel ABK, Schädel C, Schaefer K, Sky J, Subin ZM, Tarnocai C, Turetsky MR, Waldrop MP, Walter Anthony KM, Wickland KP, Wilson CJ, Zimov SA. Climatic Change (2013) 119:359–374 DOI 10.1007/s10584-013-0730-7 Lead The overall amount, rate, and form of C released to the atmosphere in a warmer world will influence the strength of the permafrost C feedback to climate change. We used a survey to quantify variability in the perception of the vulnerability of permafrost C to climate change. Experts were asked to provide quantitative estimates of permafrost change in response to four scenarios of warming. Paper Abstract Approximately 1700 Pg of soil carbon (C) are stored in the northern circumpolar permafrost zone, more than twice as much C than in the atmosphere. The overall amount, rate, and form of C released to the atmosphere in a warmer world will influence the strength of the permafrost C feedback to climate change. We used a survey to quantify variability in the perception of the vulnerability of permafrost C to climate change. Experts were asked to provide quantitative estimates of permafrost change in response to four scenarios of warming. For the highest warming scenario (RCP 8.5), experts hypothesized that C release from permafrost zone soils could be 19–45 Pg C by 2040, 162–288 Pg C by 2100, and 381–616 Pg C by 2300 in CO2 equivalent using 100-year CH4 global warming potential (GWP). These values become 50 % larger using 20-year CH4 GWP, with a third to a half of expected climate forcing coming from CH4 even though CH4 was only 2.3 % of the expected C release. Experts projected that two thirds of this release could be avoided under the lowest warming scenario (RCP 2.6). These results highlight the potential risk from permafrost thaw and serve to frame a hypothesis about the magnitude of this feedback to climate change. However, the level of emissions proposed here are unlikely to overshadow the impact of fossil fuel burning, which will continue to be the main source of C emissions and climate forcing. Manuscript - Download paper: http://link.springer.com/article/10.1007%2Fs10584-013-0730-7#page-1 - Contact lead author: E. A. G. Schuur Illustrative Photos (available free if credited as specified). | || |---|---|---| Frozen soil sediment deposit in Siberia. | Frozen peatland soil from the Hudson Bay Lowlands, Canada. |

--- PAGE: Empirical estimates to reduce modeling uncertainties of soil organic ... ---
URL: https://www.usgs.gov/publications/empirical-estimates-reduce-modeling-uncertainties-soil-organic-carbon-permafrost
CONTENT:
Empirical estimates to reduce modeling uncertainties of soil organic carbon in permafrost regions: a review of recent progress and remaining challenges The vast amount of organic carbon (OC) stored in soils of the northern circumpolar permafrost region is a potentially vulnerable component of the global carbon cycle. However, estimates of the quantity, decomposability, and combustibility of OC contained in permafrost-region soils remain highly uncertain, thereby limiting our ability to predict the release of greenhouse gases due to permafrost thawing. Substantial differences exist between empirical and modeling estimates of the quantity and distribution of permafrost-region soil OC, which contribute to large uncertainties in predictions of carbon–climate feedbacks under future warming. Here, we identify research challenges that constrain current assessments of the distribution and potential decomposability of soil OC stocks in the northern permafrost region and suggest priorities for future empirical and modeling studies to address these challenges. Citation Information | Publication Year | 2013 | |---|---| | Title | Empirical estimates to reduce modeling uncertainties of soil organic carbon in permafrost regions: a review of recent progress and remaining challenges | | DOI | 10.1088/1748-9326/8/3/035020 | | Authors | U. Mishra, J.D. Jastrow, R. Matamala, G. Hugelius, C.D. Koven, Jennifer W. Harden, S.L. Ping, G.J. Michaelson, Z. Fan, R.M. Miller, A. D. McGuire, C. Tarnocai, P. Kuhry, W.J. Riley, K. Schaefer, E.A.G. Schuur, M.T. Jorgenson, L. D. Hinzman | | Publication Type | Article | | Publication Subtype | Journal Article | | Series Title | Environmental Research Letters | | Index ID | 70118254 | | Record Source | USGS Publications Warehouse | | USGS Organization | Volcano Science Center |

--- PAGE: Carbon release through abrupt permafrost thaw ---
URL: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41561-019-0526-0
CONTENT:
Abstract The permafrost zone is expected to be a substantial carbon source to the atmosphere, yet large-scale models currently only simulate gradual changes in seasonally thawed soil. Abrupt thaw will probably occur in <20% of the permafrost zone but could affect half of permafrost carbon through collapsing ground, rapid erosion and landslides. Here, we synthesize the best available information and develop inventory models to simulate abrupt thaw impacts on permafrost carbon balance. Emissions across 2.5 million km2 of abrupt thaw could provide a similar climate feedback as gradual thaw emissions from the entire 18 million km2 permafrost region under the warming projection of Representative Concentration Pathway 8.5. While models forecast that gradual thaw may lead to net ecosystem carbon uptake under projections of Representative Concentration Pathway 4.5, abrupt thaw emissions are likely to offset this potential carbon sink. Active hillslope erosional features will occupy 3% of abrupt thaw terrain by 2300 but emit one-third of abrupt thaw carbon losses. Thaw lakes and wetlands are methane hot spots but their carbon release is partially offset by slowly regrowing vegetation. After considering abrupt thaw stabilization, lake drainage and soil carbon uptake by vegetation regrowth, we conclude that models considering only gradual permafrost thaw are substantially underestimating carbon emissions from thawing permafrost. Modelled data that support the findings of this study are also provided in the Supplementary Data. Code availability RMD files containing full code for the three generalized abrupt thaw models are available at https://github.com/mturetsky/Abrupt-thaw-carbon-model. References Schuur, E. A. --- PAGE: Environmental Impacts from Projected Permafrost Thaw in Alaska: Defining Knowledge Gaps, Data Needs, and Research Priorities ---
URL: https://journals.ametsoc.org/view/journals/wcas/17/3/WCAS-D-24-0150.1.xml
CONTENT:
Abstract Thawing permafrost will significantly impact regulatory activities in Alaska that are conducted by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) to protect human health and the environment. Many of those activities are in vulnerable rural lands predominantly occupied by Alaska Natives. Temperatures in Alaska—the largest and northernmost of the 50 states—are increasing disproportionately relative to the global average and at a markedly higher rate than in any other state in the Nation. Most land and surface waters in Alaska are underlaid by permafrost (or perennially frozen ground), which stabilizes infrastructure, serves community needs, and shapes ecosystems with unique environmental characteristics. The Fourth National Climate Assessment estimated that 16%–24% of near-surface Alaskan permafrost could disappear by the end of this century. Permafrost thaw not only compromises critical infrastructure used in daily life (e.g., buildings, roads, and pipelines) but also alters the movement of hazardous substances within the surrounding environment. Changes to permafrost characteristics and extent may release contaminants through multiple mechanisms, which can pollute air, soil, and water, with unintended consequences to subsistence activities and the livelihood of local communities. This paper summarizes information gathered from semiformal interviews conducted with EPA regulatory staff during late 2022 to glean the breadth of activities that would be affected by permafrost thaw, tabulate available data sources, and identify data and knowledge gaps. This information can be used to influence monitoring activities and future research that can inform EPA regulatory activities affected by permafrost thaw in Alaska. Significance Statement Permafrost (or perennially frozen ground) is widespread in Alaska. It serves vital roles for communities and ecosystems, including infrastructure support, natural refrigeration, and insulation from contaminant seepage from waste disposal and other sources. Rapid warming in Alaska is compromising permafrost, which can create adverse environmental and health risks. Internal interviews at the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency revealed knowledge and data gaps to improve the understanding of the current state and future characteristics of permafrost. Regulators need to understand the extent and depth of permafrost—and the timing and changes to that permafrost—at a fine spatial resolution to inform adaptation activities in partnership with state, local, and Tribal leaders. Filling those gaps facilitates protecting the people, resources, and ecosystems in Alaska. © 2025 American Meteorological Society.
